coredata.dc:title: On-site Simultaneous Determination of Neonicotinoids, Carbamates, and Phenyl Pyrazole Insecticides in Vegetables by QuEChERS Extraction on Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped Carbon Dots and Portable Mass Spectrometry
coredata.dc:creator: Mou, Binglin
Zuo, Chengyi
Chen, La
Xie, Hongzhen
Zhang, Wentian
Wang, Qiqin
Wen, Luhong
Gan, Ning
coredata.dc:description: In food safety monitoring, on-site and simultaneous detection of a variety of insecticides with different concentrations in the same matrix is necessary. However, the task remains challenging. In this study, a novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dot (N, S-CD) was synthesized and used as a QuEChERS clean-up reagent to reduce matrix interferences in the determination of insecticides in vegetables. In addition, a portable mass spectrometer (µ-MS) was employed, without chromatography separation, to directly determine neonicotinoids, carbamates, and benzopyrazole insecticides (with acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran as models) in the pretreated samples. The N,S-CD µ-MS method exhibited effective clean-up performance with satisfactory matrix effects between −15.2% and 15.7%. The recoveries of spiked vegetable samples ranged from 82.2% to 109.7% for the five target insecticides, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranged from 3.8% to 16.5%. The linear ranges were from 2.0 to 5.0 ng/g, with low detection limits (LOD) from 0.5 to 1.0 ng/g. Moreover, the total pretreatment and detection time was within 20 min. Thus, the incorporation of N,S-CD with QuEChERS extraction, together with the portable µ-MS system, could be a promising and feasible strategy for on-site, rapid, and simultaneous detection of various insecticides in vegetables.
coredata.dcterms:subject: QUECHERS
Carbon dots
Portable mass spectrometer
Pesticide residues

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of N,S-CD, and other quechers reagents
Effect of shaking time on sample purification
Effect of temperature on sample purification
Effect of the amount of extraction salt on the extraction efficiency
Effect of acetonitrile volume on the extraction efficiency
Effect of acetonitrile volume during purification on analyte recovery
Mechanism of the n,s-cd sorbent in quechers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A diagram of the experimental procedure using N,S-CD as the purification material.

TEM characterization of N,S-CD (a). The histogram of particle size distribution of N,S-CD (b). XPS full survey of N,S-CD (c). The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CD in the presence of different concentrations of acetamiprid (d).

The chromatograms of spiked vegetable extracts treated with different clean-up materials (a) without treatment, (b) treated with C18, (c) treated with GCB, (d) treated with PSA, (e) treated with N,S-CD. Numbers 1–5 are the peaks of the pesticides thiamethoxam, imidacloprid, acetamidine, fipronil, and carbofuran, respectively.

The effect of the N,S-CD amount on the purification performance (a). Effect of shaking time on extraction efficiency (b); Effect of temperature on extraction efficiency (c); Effect of the amount of extraction salt on the extraction efficiency (d); Effect of acetonitrile volume on extraction efficiency (e); Effect of acetonitrile volume on sample purification (f).

The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CD combined with five pesticides (a), the concentration of acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran was 50 ng g −1; The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CD combined with three vegetable substrates (b); The comparison of the zeta potential of CDs synthesized from different raw materials (c); A schematic of the adsorption of N,S-CD (d).
Comparison of other methods for pesticide residue determination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of neonicotinoids, carbamates, and benzopyrazole insecticides in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Spiked, Found are in units of ng g −1; ND: Not detected.

head.ce:title: On-site Simultaneous Determination of Neonicotinoids, Carbamates, and Phenyl Pyrazole Insecticides in Vegetables by QuEChERS Extraction on Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped Carbon Dots and Portable Mass Spectrometry

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In food safety monitoring, on-site and simultaneous detection of a variety of insecticides with different concentrations in the same matrix is necessary. However, the task remains challenging. In this study, a novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dot (N, S-CD) was synthesized and used as a QuEChERS clean-up reagent to reduce matrix interferences in the determination of insecticides in vegetables. In addition, a portable mass spectrometer (µ-MS) was employed, without chromatography separation, to directly determine neonicotinoids, carbamates, and benzopyrazole insecticides (with acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran as models) in the pretreated samples. The N,S-CD µ-MS method exhibited effective clean-up performance with satisfactory matrix effects between −15.2% and 15.7%. The recoveries of spiked vegetable samples ranged from 82.2% to 109.7% for the five target insecticides, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranged from 3.8% to 16.5%. The linear ranges were from 2.0 to 5.0 ng/g, with low detection limits (LOD) from 0.5 to 1.0 ng/g. Moreover, the total pretreatment and detection time was within 20 min. Thus, the incorporation of N,S-CD with QuEChERS extraction, together with the portable µ-MS system, could be a promising and feasible strategy for on-site, rapid, and simultaneous detection of various insecticides in vegetables.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is critical for public health and has direct economic and societal effects [1]. However, in many foods, insecticide residues persist and have proven problematic. For example, neonicotinoids, carbamates, and phenyl pyrazole insecticides, such as acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran, often co-exist in vegetables [2], which are widely used as highly effective and broad-spectrum insecticides in agricultural products [3]. Moreover, vegetables are perishable; therefore, food safety determinations and inspections must be carried out quickly. As a result, it is crucially important to develop on-site, simultaneous, and rapid analytical methods for detecting pesticide residues in the field.

In recent years, enzyme inhibition test strips, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [4], and Raman spectroscopy [5] methods have been developed for the on-site detection of insecticide residues. However, most of the assays can only detect one kind of analyte or total concentration of insecticides. Mass spectrometers [6] are commonly used for the simultaneous and accurate quantification of substances, and they are well suited for accurate and highly sensitive quantification of insecticides, due to their high selectivity. However, most mass spectrometers are coupled with chromatography instruments (such as HPLC-MS or GC–MS) for the separation of the analytes in advance. Chromatographic and mass spectrometry instruments are cumbersome and not suitable for on-site, portable detection. Recently, portable mass spectrometers (µ-MS) have been developed for on-site detection of pesticide residues, with high efficiency and multiple-target capacity [7]. However, the direct injection of complex samples onto the µ-MS greatly affects the accuracy of the results, making sample pretreatment a necessary step [8]. Recently, our group developed an on-site detection method for malachite green, crystal violet, and their metabolites in fish by combining a µ-MS with bamboo stir bar sorptive extraction [9]. The bamboo stir bar consisted of an organic membrane that effectively extracted the targets and made the assay suitable for on-site detection. However, the extraction time was as long as 40 min, and the organic membrane had to be prepared before each extraction.

QuEChERS is a rapid sample pre-treatment method that is gaining increasing interest in the analysis of contaminants in foods because it is fast, easy, and effective [10–12]. QuEChERS involves the extraction of samples using organic solvents followed by purification based on dispersive solid-phase extraction. QuEChERS methods are easy and suitable for multi-residue pretreatment. In particular, the purification step is critical to the method, and the purification reagent plays an important role. It should be capable of removing matrix interference as much as possible while optimally retaining the target sample in the sampling solution. Therefore, the selection and use of purification materials are of decisive significance. Primary secondary amine (PSA) [13], octadecyl modified silicon (C18), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphitized carbon black (GCB) [14–16] are commonly used as QuEChERS purification materials. For example, Nur Cebi et al. [17] developed a QuEChERS-based HPLC-MS method for pesticide residue detection in hazelnut samples using PSA as the clean-up reagent with satisfied recovery. However for the µ-MS without chromatographic separation, we found the above sorbents were not suitable in some complex vegetable samples with complex matrix, including leek, celery, and cowpea. Therefore, the development of novel clean-up adsorbents for improving the recovery of the targets in µ-MS.

To solve these problems, researchers have developed more efficient QuEChERS purification reagents. In recent years, carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CDs), have attracted much attention. CDs are a class of quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size [18]. In recent years, CDs have been frequently employed in food safety assays due to their excellent properties, such as high light absorption, chemical stability, and low toxicity [19]. The surface of CDs can be easily grafted with hydrophilic or hydrophobic groups, giving the material advantages of high specific surface area, low toxicity, dispersibility, and stability. Therefore, CDs are one of the preferred choices for extraction materials [19]. For example, Lin et al. [20] developed a fluorescence assay for pesticide detection based on the change in fluorescent light (FL) intensity from CDs. The signal was proportional to the concentration of pesticides, with a LOD of 50 µg/kg. In addition, CD-based aptasensors have been developed to detect neonicotinoids, carbamates, and benzopyrazole insecticides. Zev S. et al. [21] prepared a simple and label-free aptamer sensor based on CDs for the selective and sensitive detection of acetamiprid. CDs have a positive charge and readily interact with negatively charged aptamers, reducing the fluorescence of CDs. Conversely, the addition of acetamiprid increased the fluorescence, indicating CDs have no adsorption effect on acetamiprid. In addition, other studies have shown CDs cannot nonspecifically adsorb the three insecticides [22,23]. Inspired by these studies, we hypothesized that CDs are promising purification reagents for QuEChERS extraction of neonicotinoids, carbamates, and benzopyrazole insecticides. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are few reports employing CDs as QuEChERS purification reagents.

PSA, as a common QuEChERS purification reagent, has numerous ethylenediamine groups, which have good purification effects. Therefore, we modified the hydrophilic groups on CDs to achieve high purification effects. In the study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CD) were prepared from urea, citric acid, and thiourea. The N,S-CDs exhibited good hydrophilicity and the ability to adsorb hydrophilic impurities in vegetables. Thus, QuEChERS pretreatment, using N,S-CD as the purification materials, was coupled with portable µ-MS for the on-site and simultaneous detection of several insecticides in vegetables (Fig. 1 ).

Agricultural residue standards, including acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran, as well as HPLC-grade acetonitrile (MeCN) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (The United States). Citric acid, urea, and thiourea were analytically pure and purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). PSA, C18, GCB, and aminated multi-walled carbon nanotubes were purchased from Sigma (The United States). Extraction solvents and salts, including anhydrous magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) and sodium acetate (CH3COONa), were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Samples of leek, celery, and cowpea vegetables were obtained from local farmers’ markets (Ningbo, China).

The N,S-Carbon dots were synthesized according to a previously published method with some revision [24]. Anhydrous citric acid (1.92 g), urea (0.6) g, and thiourea (0.76)g were dispersed in 20 mL of ultrapure water, transferred to a plastic centrifuge tube with a cap, and sonicated for 15 min to disperse the mixture uniformly. The solution was transferred to a PTFE-lined reaction vessel, and the reaction was continued for 5 h in a 180 °C drying oven. After natural cooling to room temperature, the dark-colored aqueous N,S co-doped Carbon dots solution was dialyzed in a 500–1000 D dialysis bag for 24 h to remove unreacted impurities. The dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain N,S-CD.

The vegetables were homogenized using an electric pulveriser. The crushed vegetable samples (10 g ± 0.01 g) were transferred to 50 mL plastic centrifuge tubes, and 10 mL of 0.5 mg/L standard solutions of acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran (in acetonitrile) were added. The samples were vortexed for 1 min to completely disperse the standards in the matrix. Then, 6.0 g of anhydrous magnesium sulfate and 1.5 g of sodium acetate were added to the matrix system, shaken vigorously for 1 min, and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 3 min. Next, 2 mL of the supernatant was transferred to a 10 mL polypropylene tube containing 0.5 g N,S-CD, shaken again for 3 min, and centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was used as a sample solution for MS analysis.

The portable mass spectrometer "CRAIV-110," manufactured by Ningbo Hua Yi Ning Chuang Intelligent Technology Co (Ningbo, China), was employed in this study. The sample volume was 10.0 µL; PDESI was the ion source; and ultra-pure helium (≥ 99.999%) was the carrier gas. The inlet temperature was 200 °C; the molecular pump speed was 1375 Hz; and selective ion monitoring (SIM) mode was used for quantitative detection of the analytes. Sampling was performed by manually aspirating the sample solution using a capillary needle manufactured by Huayi Ningchuang. After collecting the sample, the needle was placed vertically in front of the inlet port of the mass spectrometer and pressed in for detection. The details of the QuEChERS pretreatment and the detection process are displayed in Fig. S1. The transitions and the structures of ACE, IMI, THI, FIP, and CAR are shown in Table S1. Among them, the quantification ions of ACE, IMI, THI, FIP, and CAR were m/z 183.11, 175.45, 210.81, 332.25, and 122.87, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), the morphology of N,S-CD was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) [25], and the particle size distribution of N,S-CD was characterized by a particle size distribution histogram. According to the figure, the prepared N,S-CD has good particle dispersion and a uniform size of about 10 nm. The full scan XPS spectrum of N,S-CD is shown in Fig. 2(c) [26], and three peaks appeared at 531, 400, and 284 eV, which corresponded to O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s, respectively. An additional peak at 164 eV was observed in a full investigation of N,S-CD, which was attributed to S 2p. This indicates the CDs were successfully doped with N and S. Most of the N,S-CDs consisted of carbon (61.8%), followed by oxygen (29.6%), nitrogen (8%), and sulfur (0.6%). FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2(d)) was used to identify the surface functional groups on N,S-CD. The wide absorption band at 3428 cm−1 was ascribed to the tensile vibrations of OH and NH [27]. This shows N,S-CDs possessed numerous amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface. The smaller peak value at 2071 cm−1 was the vibration absorption band of CN in thiocyanate (-SCN). The peak at 1724 cm−1 was the vibration absorption band of C = O in CONH2, and 1620 cm−1 was the vibration absorption band of C = C and C = N. The bending vibration band of CH was observed at 1401 cm−1. The peak values of N,S-CD at 1092 cm−1 were attributed to CN and CO tensile vibrations. A weak C-S contraction vibration was observed from 747 cm−1 to 892 cm−1. The results demonstrate there were abundant hydrophilic groups on the N,S-CD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of N,S-CD, and other quechers reagents

ce:section.ce:para: The full-scan chromatograms of vegetable substrates treated with different purification materials and spiked with five representative pesticides are shown in Fig 3 . PSA, GCB, and C18 were used as comparison materials. The five pesticides labeled 1–5 in Fig. 3 are thiamethoxam, imidacloprid, acetamidine, fipronil, and carbofuran, respectively. From Fig. 3, many interfering peaks in the vegetable substrate without pre-treatment (curve (a)) were observed, and the impurity peaks were very high, which can adversely affect the accuracy of pesticide detection. This result justifies the need for sample pretreatment [28]. Compared with curve (a), the impurity peaks in curve (b) (C18) were reduced, but the peaks of the target pesticides were also much lower. This is because C18 is a non-selective material that adsorbs all substances without selectivity. The pesticide peaks in the chromatograms purified by GCB (c), PSA (d), and N,S-CD (e) were significantly higher, indicating the recovery rate of the target analytes was higher. In contrast, the lowest peak and the highest pesticide peak in curve by N,S-CD (e), where most of the interference was effectively re-removed, indicated that the purification performance of N,S-CD was better than the other three clean-up reagents.

The recoveries and RSDs were calculated at a spiked level of 0.5 mg/kg, and the results are shown in Table S2. When C18 was used as the purifying agent, the recoveries ranged from 62.1% to 99.1%, and the RSDs ranged from 5.9 to 30.1%, indicating C18 was not effective in removing interferences in the analysis. When PSA was used, the recoveries ranged from 62.3% to 92.3%, with RSDs of 6.6–32.4%, indicating PSA was also not an effective purifying agent. When GCB was used as the purifying agent, the recoveries ranged from 81.6% to 95.6%, with RSDs of 10.8% to 20.5%, indicating GCB was a better option than PSA and C18. However, the best results were obtained when N,S-CD was used, in terms of recovery (from 77.8% to 101%) and RSDs (≤ 17.4%). These results demonstrate the use of N,S-CD as an effective and selective QuEChERS clean-up reagent for the detection of neonicotinoids, carbamates, and benzopyrazole pesticide residues in vegetables.

N,S-CD was used as QuEChERS clean-up material for acetonitrile extracts of vegetables. The spiked recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran at 0.5 mg/kg were determined, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). At the same time, we also made a comparison of the amount of PSA used as clean-up material. The comparison plot of PSA dosage optimization is presented in Fig S3. When the amount of N,S-CD was 100 mg, the recovery was less than 80%, which was below the qualified recovery range [29]. When the amount was 300 mg and 500 mg, the recoveries were 85.5%–107.6% and 82.2%–109.7%, respectively, which were not much different. However, the RSDs were 12.3%–24.6% at 300 mg and 3.8%–16.5% at 500 mg, which was more in line with the requirements of the analysis. As the amount of N,S-CD increased to 800 mg and 1000 mg, the recoveries of most pesticides showed a decreasing trend. This may be because N,S-CD also exerted some adsorption capacity towards the organic insecticides due to electrostatic interactions, van der Waals forces, and π-π stacking effects. When the amount of N,S-CD was too high, they can compete with acetonitrile to adsorb some targets on it, leading to the recovery decrease. In Fig S3, the recoveries of the target analytes using PSA are compared with N,S-CD. With an increasing amount of PSA, a proportion of the target analytes were adsorbed, and the recovery decreased. However, compared with PSA (33.8% at 800 mg and 18.3% at 1000 mg), it is obvious that the recovery decrement of N,S-CD (48.1% at 800 mg and 40.87% at 1000 mg) is smaller. Therefore, 500 mg was chosen as the optimum amount of N,S-CD in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of shaking time on sample purification
ce:section.ce:para: To clearly describe the effect of shaking time on sample purification, experiments were carried out at 25 °C with shaking times of 1, 3, 5, 8, and 10 min, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(b). The shaking time had little effect on the extraction of target pesticides. To prevent error in practice, 3 min was chosen as the optimal shaking time for this experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on sample purification
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on the efficiency of N,S-CD in eliminating impurities from vegetable samples was investigated by varying the temperature from 10 °C to 35 °C. The results are shown in Fig. 4(c). When the temperature was increased from 10 °C to 35 °C, there was no significant change in the extraction efficiency of N,S-CD, which is beneficial to on-site applications of this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the amount of extraction salt on the extraction efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the amount of anhydrous magnesium sulfate used in the extraction on the recovery of target pesticides is shown in Fig. 4(d). When the amount of anhydrous magnesium sulfate increased from 2 g to 6 g, the recovery of pesticides from the samples steadily increased, reaching a maximum of 6 g. However, it should be noted that the anhydrous magnesium sulfate had a certain adsorption effect on the five pesticides, and the recoveries in the sample solutions decreased continuously when the anhydrous magnesium sulfate amount increased from 6 g to 10 g. Therefore, 6 g was selected as the optimal amount of anhydrous magnesium sulfate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of acetonitrile volume on the extraction efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of acetonitrile volume was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(e). When the volume of acetonitrile used during extraction was less than 10 mL, the recovery rates were very low. In contrast, when the volume of acetonitrile was more than 10 mL, the recovery rates were stable. Based on the principle of green chemistry, 10 mL was selected as the optimal volume for extraction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of acetonitrile volume during purification on analyte recovery
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the volume of acetonitrile on the recovery of target analytes was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(f). As the volume of acetonitrile increased, the recovery increased and then stabilized at 2 mL. Therefore, 2 mL was the optimal volume of acetonitrile used during purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the n,s-cd sorbent in quechers
ce:section.ce:para: In the fluorescence spectrum of N,S-CD containing the target analytes at a concentration of 50 ng g −1 (Fig. 5 (a)), the signals of the five pesticides (ACE, IMI, THI, FIP, and CAR) hardly decreased after the purification step. The results show N,S-CD did not adsorb the pesticides in the detection range. The fluorescence spectrum of N,S-CD containing the vegetable matrix (Fig. 5 b) demonstrates the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CD was largely quenched (70–90%) by the matrix. Therefore, matrix compounds in the vegetable samples can be adsorbed by N,S-CD, inhibiting the fluorescence signal of N,S-CD. According to the literature, hydrophilic groups, such as -SCN, -OH, and -NH2 groups, are formed when thiourea and urea are carbonized at high temperature [30]. Thus, N,S-CD exhibited excellent water solubility, due to the presence of these hydrophilic groups. The five insecticides have good water-solubility. Fig 5 (c) shows the zeta potentials of CDs synthesized using citric acid alone, citric acid and urea, citric acid and thiourea, as well as N,S-CD and PSA (the common QuEChERS purification material), respectively. The pure citric acid-based CDs exhibited positive charges, while CDs formed with urea and thiourea combined with citric acid showed negative charges. In addition, PSA and N,S-CD are both positively charged, and N,S-CD has more positive charges than PSA. This may be due to the many -NH2 groups on the surface of N,S-CD, which can be hydrolyzed to -NH3 + at neutral pH. Moreover, the pKa values of ACE, IMI, THI, FIP, and CAR are close to or higher than 7.0. Therefore, they exhibit neutral or positive charges in a neutral pH environment. As a result, the positively charged N,S-CD can repel the positively charged insecticides by electrostatic forces. All of these factors can contribute to the weak adsorption ability of N,S-CD towards the five water-soluble insecticides.

According to the FT-IR spectrum (Fig 2(d)), zeta potential diagram (Fig 5 (c)), and previous reports [30,31], we speculated that the grafted groups on the N,S-CD surface are shown in Fig 5 (d). All these are similar to PSA. Therefore, N,S-CD can exhibit strong binding ability with macromolecular substances, such as pigments, organic acids, cellulose, and polysaccharides, due to van der Waals attraction and other hydrophobic interactions.

In mass spectrometry, matrix effects negatively impact the accuracy and precision of the method. In response, an important feature of the QuEChERS method is the reduction of matrix effects by removing interfering substances. To investigate the ability of N,S-CD to reduce matrix interference, six sets of calibration curves were constructed, including a standard curve, a calibration curve of the untreated vegetable matrix, and a calibration curve of the vegetable matrix purified by C18, PSA, GCB, and N,S-CD, respectively. The matrix effect (ME) can be calculated by using Eq. (1) according to previous methods [32,33].
(1)
ME =
(
1
slope
of
calibration
curve
in
matrix
slope
of
calibration
curve
in
solvent
)
× 100 %

As shown in Table 1 , in the untreated samples, the matrix effects of the five pesticides were high, ranging from –42.2% to 62.3%. After pretreatment with N,S-CD, the matrix effects ranged from –15.2% to 15.7%, and compared with other purification materials (C18 from 16.5% to 57.8%, PSA from 10.7% to 50.5%, GCB from 7%−41.6%), N,S-CD reduced the matrix effect in the detection of three types of pesticides more. This result further demonstrates the superior purification effects of N,S-CD, compared to PSA and GCBs. Thus, N,S-CD is a promising sorbent material for QuEChERS extraction and the pretreatment of complex samples for µ-MS detection.

The analytical performance of the method, including the correlation coefficient (R), limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), relative standard deviation (RSDs), and working curves for quantification of ionic strength, was validated under optimal conditions. The working curves were plotted by spiking 20–500 ng/g of each pesticide to cowpea, leek, and celery samples and using N,S-CD as the QuEChERS sorbent material. LOD and LOQ were determined by signal-to-noise ratios (S/N) of 3 and 10, respectively. As shown in Table S3, there was a good linear response for all targets, with R values ranging from 0.9957 to 0.9995 for LODs of 0.5 to 1.0 ng/g and LOQs of 1.3 to 4.2 ng/g. In addition, the linear range, LODs, and LOQs were stable in the three vegetable substrates (leek, celery, and cowpea).

The present method was compared with other reported methods (Table 2 ). Considering M-F-MWCNTs-based QuEChERS-GC–MS [33], MWCNTs-based QuEChERS-UPLC-MS [34], PSA- and GCB-based QuEChERS-GC–MS [35], UiO-66-based d-SPE-GC–MS [36], and Fe3O4@TFNCDPs-based MSPE-HPLC [37] methods, the LOD and linear range of the current QuEChERS-MS method based on N,S-CD are comparable, but the time required by other methods is more than 15 min, which is not suitable for rapid, in-field detection. Therefore, the developed method performed well in terms of extraction time, linear range, LOD, and RSDs. This is a simple, sensitive, and rapid method for the analysis of pesticide residues in vegetables.

The established method was used to detect neonicotinoid insecticides (acetamiprid, imidacloprid, and thiamethoxam), carbamates (carbofuran), and benzopyrazole (fipronil) in cowpea, celery, and leek samples. The results are listed in Table 3 . The accuracy of the method was verified by performing recovery tests at two spiked concentration levels. Acetamiprid, fipronil, and carbofuran were detected in the cowpea substrate. Only imidacloprid was detected in the leek matrix, and acetamiprid, imidacloprid, and carbofuran were detected in the celery matrix. The concentrations detected were within the permitted limits for use. The recoveries were 70.3%–111.4%, 70.4%–108.2%, and 79.6%–108.1% for spiked cowpea, leek, and celery, respectively, which confirmed the satisfactory accuracy of the method. The mass spectra of the samples spiked with 0.5 mg/kg were detected on the µ-MS, as shown in Fig. S2. Fipronil was detected in negative ion mode, and the rest were detected in positive ion mode.

A novel N,S-CD sorbent based on the carbonization of urea, citric acid, and thiourea was prepared and used in QuEChERS pretreatment of vegetable samples. N,S-CD effectively removed matrix interferences from the complex samples. Compared to PSA, C18, and GCB, N,S-CD exhibited a superior clean-up performance. Through the combination of N,S-CD-based QuEChERS with a portable mass spectrometer (µ-MS), the assay was successfully applied to on-site, rapid, and simultaneous detection of five kinds of familiar insecticide residues (acetamiprid, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, fipronil, and carbofuran) in vegetable samples. The experimental results demonstrated the improved QuEChERS-µ-MS method was simple, fast, and suitable for simultaneous detection of insecticides in food safety monitoring.

Binglin Mou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Chengyi Zuo: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. La Chen: Validation, Formal analysis. Hongzhen Xie: Writing – review & editing. Wentian Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Qiqin Wang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Luhong Wen: Software, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration. Ning Gan: Data curation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974074); Zhejiang Provincial Key Research and Development Program (No. 2020C02023, Y23B050013); Zhejiang Province Public Welfare Technology Application Research Plan (Y23C200022, LY20B050004, LG20B050006); Ningbo ‘Jie Bang Gua Shuai’ major research project (2022S011, 20211ZDYF020179), Guangdong Panyu District Innovation Team (2017-R01–5), Wang Kuancheng Happiness Fund of Ningbo University and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2022.463744.
